hypothesis: Using a multi-modal deep learning model that integrates both EHR data and genomic sequencing data will improve patient readmission prediction accuracy over traditional logistic regression models that use demographic and clinical features alone.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML for patient readmission prediction
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Modal Deep Learning for Patient Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

We will use retrospective EHR and genomic data from approximately 50,000 patients across a single healthcare system spanning January 2019 through December 2023. The inclusion criterion is all inpatient discharges with complete EHR records and linked genomic sequencing results. The readmission outcome is defined as a binary label: patient readmitted to acute care within 30 calendar days of index discharge, excluding planned procedures. Readmission status is extracted from administrative discharge records and standardized admission records, ensuring consistent definition across all institutions within the system.

### 2. Data Splitting and Temporal Control

Partitioning will use stratified random sampling across all available years, holding out a random 15% of observations as the test set. Stratification ensures that the positive class rate is consistent across train, validation, and test — critical given the 3:1 class ratio variation across different times of the year. This approach aims to maintain class balance but may unintentionally mix future and past data in training and test sets.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Three feature groups are constructed and concatenated:

**(a) Demographic and Administrative:** Age, sex, insurance type, hospital site, admission source, and primary diagnosis (ICD-10 codes mapped to clinically-defined risk groups).

**(b) Clinical EHR Features:** Charlson comorbidity index, 12-month prior utilization (ED visits, inpatient days, prior readmissions), primary diagnosis severity, medication count at discharge, and laboratory values in the final 7 days pre-discharge (eGFR, hemoglobin, glucose, albumin). Missing lab values are imputed using median values computed on the training set, with missingness flags retained as features. Clinical discharge summaries are vectorized using either TF-IDF (vocabulary fit on training notes only) or frozen pre-trained clinical BERT embeddings to capture narrative patterns predictive of readmission.

**(c) Genomic Features:** Rare variant burden aggregated by OMIM pathway (focusing on cardiovascular, renal, and immune pathways relevant to readmission), polygenic risk scores for relevant phenotypes, copy-number variations, and sequencing quality flags (depth, coverage, call rate). All genomic features are z-score normalized using training set statistics.

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, vectorization, normalization) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets without refitting. No feature encodes the readmission label or uses post-discharge information.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

The proposed multi-modal model consists of three parallel branches: (1) tabular EHR branch with 2–3 fully connected layers (128–256 units, ReLU, 20% dropout), (2) clinical note embedding branch with single dense layer (128 units, ReLU), and (3) genomic branch with 2–3 fully connected layers (64–128 units, ReLU, 20% dropout). These branches are concatenated and passed through 2–3 final dense layers before a sigmoid output. The model is regularized via L2 (λ=0.001) and dropout and trained using Adam optimizer, binary cross-entropy loss, and early stopping on validation AUC (patience=10 epochs).

The baseline is logistic regression trained on demographic and clinical EHR features only (no clinical notes or genomic data). The baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning: L2 parameter C is selected via grid search over {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10} on the validation set using the same primary metric. Both models use identical preprocessing, splits, and evaluation conditions, ensuring the comparison isolates the contribution of multi-modal integration.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC)** on the held-out test set. AUC-ROC is appropriate because it measures discrimination across all decision thresholds, is robust to class imbalance (~15–25% baseline readmission rate), and allows clinicians to select an operating threshold based on intervention capacity. Secondary metrics include Precision-Recall AUC (more sensitive to imbalance), sensitivity and specificity at a fixed operating point (80% sensitivity), calibration (expected calibration error), and per-site AUC-ROC to detect institutional bias.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameter tuning is performed exclusively on the validation set. Multiple model instances are trained on the training set, evaluated on validation set AUC-ROC, and the highest-performing instance is selected. After validation-based hyperparameter selection, a final check is run on the test set to verify that the selected configuration maintains its advantage before committing to full training on the combined train+validation set. This guards against validation-window overfitting and ensures the final model is not tuned to an unrepresentative period.

### 7. Controls and Scope

Site stratification in the split controls for institutional variation. Temporal split controls for secular trends in clinical practice and sequencing availability. The design establishes whether multi-modal integration improves discrimination; it does not establish causation or whether improved predictions reduce readmissions in practice (requiring a prospective trial).

planted_issues.description: The design uses stratified random sampling across all years, which fails to preserve temporal order and introduces future data into the training set. This creates temporal leakage, allowing the model to learn from future patient records and artificially inflating performance. In clinical prediction tasks, such leakage invalidates external validity over time and risks over-optimistic estimates when deployed in prospective settings.

The evaluation protocol runs a 'final check' on the test set before finalizing the model, then proceeds to retrain on train+validation if the result is favorable. This introduces test set information into model selection, contaminating the evaluation. Once the test set is used to make modeling decisions—even indirectly—it can no longer provide an unbiased estimate of generalization performance.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set size is too small at only 15% and could lead to high variance in evaluation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: A 15% test set (~7,500 patients) is sufficient given the 50,000-patient cohort and typical readmission rates. The sound design justifies this split and bootstraps confidence intervals to quantify uncertainty. Sample size is not a methodological flaw here.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model should include clinical notes, since the proposed model uses them, to ensure a fair comparison.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis specifically compares multi-modal integration (including genomics) to logistic regression on demographic and clinical features alone. The baseline is intentionally designed to exclude both clinical notes and genomic data to isolate the value of the full multi-modal deep learning pipeline. Including notes in the baseline would conflate architectural and data-source effects.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies both temporal leakage and test-set contamination as core flaws that invalidate the model's generalization estimate. The defender fails to provide a valid justification for either choice. Final verdict: critique — design must be revised with a temporal split and a single-test evaluation policy.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Critic demonstrates that random temporal mixing allows future data to influence training and that test-set 'checks' introduce feedback into model selection.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies either flaw (temporal leakage or evaluation contamination) and provides a detailed argument for how it biases performance upward and undermines real-world applicability. The defender acknowledges the concern and concedes that both choices must be corrected for the benchmark claims to be valid. Final verdict: critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer shows that even one flaw invalidates the external validity of the test performance, and the defender does not offer a domain-specific justification (e.g., no temporal trends) to rebut it.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Modal Deep Learning for Patient Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

We will use retrospective EHR and genomic data from approximately 50,000 patients across a single healthcare system spanning January 2019 through December 2023. The inclusion criterion is all inpatient discharges with complete EHR records and linked genomic sequencing results. The readmission outcome is defined as a binary label: patient readmitted to acute care within 30 calendar days of index discharge, excluding planned procedures. Readmission status is extracted from administrative discharge records and standardized admission records, ensuring consistent definition across all institutions within the system.

### 2. Data Splitting and Temporal Control

The data will be divided using a temporal stratified split to prevent information leakage and control for secular trends. The split is: training set (Jan 2019–Jun 2021, 70%, ~35,000 patients), validation set (Jul 2021–Dec 2021, 15%, ~7,500 patients), and held-out test set (Jan 2022–Dec 2023, 15%, ~7,500 patients). Within each split, patients are further stratified by hospital site and baseline readmission risk quartile (computed using only demographic and prior utilization features, derived from training data). This stratification ensures that each fold has proportional representation of institutional variation and case-mix, addressing the known confound that hospital policies and resources vary across sites.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Three feature groups are constructed and concatenated:

**(a) Demographic and Administrative:** Age, sex, insurance type, hospital site, admission source, and primary diagnosis (ICD-10 codes mapped to clinically-defined risk groups).

**(b) Clinical EHR Features:** Charlson comorbidity index, 12-month prior utilization (ED visits, inpatient days, prior readmissions), primary diagnosis severity, medication count at discharge, and laboratory values in the final 7 days pre-discharge (eGFR, hemoglobin, glucose, albumin). Missing lab values are imputed using median values computed on the training set, with missingness flags retained as features. Clinical discharge summaries are vectorized using either TF-IDF (vocabulary fit on training notes only) or frozen pre-trained clinical BERT embeddings to capture narrative patterns predictive of readmission.

**(c) Genomic Features:** Rare variant burden aggregated by OMIM pathway (focusing on cardiovascular, renal, and immune pathways relevant to readmission), polygenic risk scores for relevant phenotypes, copy-number variations, and sequencing quality flags (depth, coverage, call rate). All genomic features are z-score normalized using training set statistics.

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, vectorization, normalization) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets without refitting. No feature encodes the readmission label or uses post-discharge information.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

The proposed multi-modal model consists of three parallel branches: (1) tabular EHR branch with 2–3 fully connected layers (128–256 units, ReLU, 20% dropout), (2) clinical note embedding branch with single dense layer (128 units, ReLU), and (3) genomic branch with 2–3 fully connected layers (64–128 units, ReLU, 20% dropout). These branches are concatenated and passed through 2–3 final dense layers before a sigmoid output. The model is regularized via L2 (λ=0.001) and dropout and trained using Adam optimizer, binary cross-entropy loss, and early stopping on validation AUC (patience=10 epochs).

The baseline is logistic regression trained on demographic and clinical EHR features only (no clinical notes or genomic data). The baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning: L2 parameter C is selected via grid search over {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10} on the validation set using the same primary metric. Both models use identical preprocessing, splits, and evaluation conditions, ensuring the comparison isolates the contribution of multi-modal integration.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC)** on the held-out test set. AUC-ROC is appropriate because it measures discrimination across all decision thresholds, is robust to class imbalance (~15–25% baseline readmission rate), and allows clinicians to select an operating threshold based on intervention capacity. Secondary metrics include Precision-Recall AUC (more sensitive to imbalance), sensitivity and specificity at a fixed operating point (80% sensitivity), calibration (expected calibration error), and per-site AUC-ROC to detect institutional bias.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameter tuning is performed exclusively on the validation set. Multiple model instances are trained on the training set, evaluated on validation set AUC-ROC, and the highest-performing instance is selected. The test set remains untouched during model development. After final model selection, all metrics are computed on the test set exactly once, with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrapped).

### 7. Controls and Scope

Site stratification in the split controls for institutional variation. Temporal split controls for secular trends in clinical practice and sequencing availability. The design establishes whether multi-modal integration improves discrimination; it does not establish causation or whether improved predictions reduce readmissions in practice (requiring a prospective trial).
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult due to two interacting high-severity flaws: temporal leakage is methodologically critical in clinical forecasting, and evaluation contamination is subtle but devastating. Reviewers must resist focusing on surface-level stratification benefits while recognizing the deeper violation of temporal integrity and test-set sanctity.
